description: 4x4 substitution matrix Pisot search for alpha in (2,3)

phase3.alpha_distribution_note: All apparent (2,3) hits have alpha numerically equal to 2.000 or 3.000 (within 0.005). No truly interior matrices found.

theoretical_analysis.conclusion: The (2,3) gap is inaccessible for 4x4 integer Pisot matrices with row sums <= 4
theoretical_analysis.reason_non_invertible: Matrices with det=0 are effectively lower-dimensional (3x3 or 2x2 embedded in 4x4). The active 3x3 block has the unimodular constraint, giving alpha=2 exactly for complex-conjugate pairs, or alpha=3 for real second eigenvalue pairs.
theoretical_analysis.reason_invertible_unimodular: For invertible 4x4 matrices, |det| = product of |eigenvalues|. For unimodular (|det|=1): lam1 * lam2 * lam3 * lam4 = 1. If alpha in (2,3): |lam2| in (1/lam1, 1/sqrt(lam1)). Then lam3*lam4 = 1/(lam1*lam2) > 1/(lam1 * 1/lam1) = 1, forcing at least one of lam3, lam4 > 1 (violating Pisot). For 2real+1cc with complex pair as second-largest: alpha = 2 + ln(lam2_real)/ln(lam1) < 2 always. THEOREM: No unimodular 4x4 Pisot matrix gives alpha in (2,3).
theoretical_analysis.reason_invertible_non_unimodular: Non-unimodular (|det| >= 2) matrices with non-negative integer entries require large row sums to keep lam2 < 1. For row sums <= 4: no Pisot matrix with alpha in (2,3) found. First known 2x2 example: M=[[0,1],[2,4]] (row sums 1 and 6) gives alpha=2.071. This requires max row sum >= 6 even in 2x2 case.
what_is_needed: 5x5 or higher matrices, where complex conjugate pairs are no longer the only structure
Non-unimodular matrices with row sums >= 6 (even in 2x2 case)
Four-symbol substitutions with at least one row summing to >= 6

smallest_known_gap_example.note: Requires max row sum = 6, well beyond the searched range

gap_proven_inaccessible_for: 3x3 matrices with row sums <= 4 (proved and verified)
4x4 matrices with row sums <= 4 (numerically verified, theory for unimodular case)
4x4 unimodular matrices of any row sum (algebraic proof)
